data.title: Grim Fandango: Remastered
data.body:
Gameplay: 6.5/10

Considering that Grim Fandango was released over 20 years ago (1998), my review of its gameplay is relatively harsh, as the adventure puzzler genre was much less developed back then. However, when compared to modern puzzlers such as Lumino City and Portal where puzzles require more intuitive and involved thinking, Grim Fandango feels underdeveloped. Most puzzles are solved by applying random objects to other random objects, which can make it extremely hard to piece together what to do next, especially in a fairly large and complex map. However, this type of problem solving is a key feature of point-and-click games, which is perhaps why this genre has “fallen” as many online gaming enthusiasts proclaim. The repetitive nature of these puzzles greatly reduced my enjoyment of the gameplay. I will give the writers credit for making many of the solutions creative and entertaining. Whether it was extinguishing fire beavers or getting goofy demons to chug barrels of alcohol, I had a lot of fun seeing these amusing moments play out. The writers also did a great job of tying these obscure puzzles to the progression of the storyline once solved. However, when strictly looking at the mechanics and puzzle solving techniques, the gameplay was a bit disappointing.

I did discover one bug where I had dropped an ax at the border of the game window between two scenes. The arrows used to walk between the screens would appear over the symbol to pick up the ax. In the end, I wasn’t able to pick up the ax, so I was forced to reload a save that set me back 15 minutes of progress. Bugs of this nature could have been avoided had a separate mechanic been created to move between screens.

Game setting and story: 9.5/10

Although I didn’t fully enjoy the gameplay itself, my expectations for the setting and story were exceeded. Double Fine did such a great job of creating a depiction of the underworld with an extremely strong personality and rich storyline. Grim Fandango has a nostalgic element to it that makes you miss the Eighth Underworld as soon as you leave it. The player can’t help but root for Manny, the main character with a witty charm and a pure heart under his initially sleazy appearance. The writers play around with the “good girl gone bad” transformation of Meche, and they successfully create villains that make your insides crawl with their slimy appearances and demeanors. They created the most annoying character possible, Glottis, to make obnoxious car revving noises in the background while you’re already irritated by being stuck on puzzles. Yet somehow, you still end up adoring Glottis by the end. The vibrant variety of characters combined with the film noir-inspired setting constructs an immersive, fully fleshed out underworld. I was initially surprised by how grainy the graphics are, but I was never bothered by the low quality since the art style is so unique and expressive.

The plotline was my favorite part of the game. My curiosity towards finding out what happens next was the greatest motivator to sticking out the endless clicking to solve puzzles. Usually in adventure games I find myself bored by copious amounts of dry dialogue. Conversely, Grim Fandango did an amazing job of making side dialogue funny, entertaining, and not too drawn out. I even looked forward to clicking on new characters and going through the entire dialogue tree. Grim Fandango’s storyline had the perfect balance of danger, romance, friendship, and betrayal to keep the player wanting more.

Overall rating: 8.5/10

After playing Grim Fandango, I fully understand the hype around it. It truly is a one-of-a-kind game. For those who care more about exciting gameplay, such as in first person shooters or RPGs, Grim Fandango may not be your cup of tea. However, for those who can appreciate games for their storyline and artform, I highly recommend playing it.

For those who are reading this review and have already finished this game, I have a few final words:

“This little light of mine, I’m gonna let it shine.” ☺
